name: DJI Mini 3 Pro Drone with RC-N1 Remote
tag: Best Compact Drone with Obstacle Sensing
introText: The DJI Mini 3 Pro was DJI's benchmark sub-250g drone before the Mini 4 Pro — 249g, 1/1.3-inch sensor, 4K/60fps 10-bit, tri-directional obstacle sensing, and vertical shooting orientation for social media content.

specs.Vertical Shooting: Yes — sensor rotates for portrait orientation

whatWorks: 249g weight is the critical number in Canada — sub-250g drones qualify for basic operations rules under Transport Canada, meaning recreational flyers can fly in many more locations without advanced certification. This practical advantage is the Mini 3 Pro's most important feature.
4K/60fps 10-bit with a 1/1.3-inch sensor at 249g is technically impressive — the image quality rivals much heavier drones. 10-bit log footage gives videographers real colour grading flexibility.

Vertical shooting orientation is native — the camera physically rotates 90° for portrait-mode video, which produces true vertical footage for Instagram Reels and TikTok rather than a cropped horizontal signal.
Strong resale ecosystem — the Mini 3 Pro has been on the market long enough to have excellent third-party accessory support and a robust used market if you want to upgrade later.
worthKnowing: DJI O3 transmission vs O4 in the Mini 4 Pro — shorter range and slightly less interference resistance. In urban environments with heavy WiFi congestion, O3 can occasionally show video feed drops where O4 would maintain connection.

Tri-directional obstacle avoidance only covers forward, backward, and downward — the sides are unprotected. Flying in tight spaces like forests requires more pilot attention than omnidirectional systems.

Mini 4 Pro is the current model at a similar price point — with omnidirectional obstacle avoidance, better transmission, and slightly longer flight time. The Mini 3 Pro is still available new at some retailers as a budget option.
34-minute flight time is good but noticeably shorter than the Air 3's 46 minutes — plan accordingly for longer shoots.

commonComplaints.title: Tri-directional sensing leaves side vulnerability…
commonComplaints.body: Tri-directional sensing leaves the sides unprotected — flying near trees, wires, or buildings requires constant attention to lateral clearance, which the Mini 4 Pro's omnidirectional system handles automatically.

commonComplaints.title: Mini 4 Pro is now similarly priced…
commonComplaints.body: Mini 4 Pro is now similarly priced and more capable — buyers should compare current prices, as the newer model's omnidirectional sensing and O4 transmission often justify the small premium.

commonComplaints.title: DJI O3 range shorter than O4…
commonComplaints.body: DJI O3 transmission range and interference resistance is lower than O4 — in congested urban areas, the video feed can degrade at distances where O4 would remain stable.

verdict: The Mini 3 Pro is still an excellent drone, particularly if you find it priced significantly below the Mini 4 Pro. The 249g weight advantage for Canadian recreational flyers is real and valuable. However, if prices are close, the Mini 4 Pro's omnidirectional obstacle sensing and O4 transmission are meaningful upgrades. Check current pricing before deciding.

realBuyers.quote: The sub-250g advantage for flying in Canada is huge. I can fly recreationally in so many more places without the advanced certification.
Vertical shooting mode is legitimately useful. My Instagram Reels look so much better than when I was cropping horizontal footage.
Image quality punches way above its size. You'd never guess a 249g drone could produce footage this clean.

sources.title: DJI Mini 3 Pro vs Mini 4 Pro — Reddit (r/drones)
DJI Mini 3 Pro Review — YouTube (Drone Supremacy)

metaDescription: DJI Mini 3 Pro review for Canada: 249g, 1/1.3-inch sensor, 4K/60fps 10-bit, vertical shooting, tri-directional obstacle sensing. ~$959 CAD on Amazon.ca.
